title: Emergency Care for Dog Open Fractures: Immediate Steps and Triage
meta_desc: Seeing a bone through your dog's skin is a critical emergency. Learn why an open fracture requires immediate vet care and how a photo helps triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: An open fracture is a critical medical emergency where bone is exposed through the skin, requiring immediate veterinary care to prevent severe infection and life-threatening complications.

What is an open fracture in pets?

An open fracture, also known as a compound fracture, is a severe injury where a broken bone has either pierced through the skin or is exposed to the outside environment through a deep wound. Unlike a closed fracture, where the skin remains intact, an open fracture is a complex trauma that affects the bone, skin, muscles, and blood vessels simultaneously.

Is an open fracture considered a veterinary emergency?

  • Yes, this is a high-level emergency. If you see bone or a deep wound at the site of a suspected break, seek veterinary care at the nearest emergency clinic immediately.
  • Open fractures are incredibly painful and carry a very high risk of life-threatening complications.
  • Potential risks include severe hemorrhage, systemic infection (sepsis), and bone infections that can lead to limb loss if not treated within the first few hours.

How does sending a photo help with veterinary triage for a fracture?

  • A clear photo allows the triage team to assess the level of contamination and tissue damage before you arrive.
  • It helps the medical team prepare stabilization equipment and wound flushing supplies in advance.
  • Staff can have heavy pain management protocols ready the moment you walk through the door.
  • Only take a picture if you can do so safely without causing your dog further pain or distress.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cases of open fractures in dogs, instruct the owner during the initial telephone call to provide support during transport, particularly for fractures below the elbow or stifle with significant displacement, to prevent further damage to muscle, nerve, vessels, or bone. This can be achieved by creating a temporary splint using a rolled newspaper or magazine secured with fabric or duct tape after adequate restraint. Triage involves a rapid assessment to identify life-threatening airway, breathing, and circulation problems, initiating immediate treatment while prioritizing patients based on historical and physical parameters, including known or suspected trauma and open wounds exposing extensive soft tissue or bone.

Chapter: Emergency, General Principles

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1659)

faqs.question: Should I try to push the bone back under the skin?
faqs.answer: No. Never attempt to manipulate the bone or push it back into the wound. Doing so can cause further nerve damage, introduce more bacteria into the body, and cause your dog extreme pain, which may lead to accidental biting.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog human pain relief while we are on the way?
faqs.answer: No. Never give your dog human medications like Ibuprofen, Tylenol, or Aspirin. These are toxic to dogs and can cause organ failure or interfere with the medications the veterinarian needs to use for emergency surgery.

faqs.question: How should I transport my dog with a broken leg?
faqs.answer: Keep your dog as still as possible. Use a flat surface like a sturdy piece of cardboard or a thick blanket as a stretcher to minimize movement of the limb. Gently cover the wound with a clean, dry cloth or sterile gauze if possible, but do not apply a tight bandage.
